accordion.description: Accordion is the secondary-disclosure primitive for stacked content inside the current page flow. Use it when details should stay nearby without competing as primary layout or primary navigation.

multiCard.description: Allow multiple items to remain open only when comparison or simultaneous review is genuinely useful.

filters.description: Useful when users need to inspect several configuration groups at the same time.

history.description: Use it when independent sections may all deserve attention during the same task.

visibility.description: Multi-open mode reduces unnecessary toggling when the user is reviewing related details.

items.disclosure: Use accordion when each section can be understood from its trigger label before the body is opened.
items.multi: Use `type="single"` when sections should compete for attention. Use `type="multiple"` when users may need to compare or keep several sections open together.
items.secondary: Treat accordion as secondary disclosure inside sections, cards, and settings groups. Do not use it as primary navigation or primary page switching.
items.trigger: Trigger labels should be short and descriptive enough for users to decide whether the section is relevant before expanding it.

roles.description: Use an accordion item when supporting ownership details should be reachable without permanently occupying the layout.

default.description: Brand primary action with no overhead label by default.

warning.description: Use warning when the action is meaningful and cautionary, but not fully destructive.

alertDialog.description: Alert dialog follows one fixed confirmation pattern: required title, optional short supporting description, variant-controlled overhead, and a footer with exactly two actions.

danger.description: Deleting permanently removes this record and cannot be undone.
danger.title: Delete this record?

default.description: Confirm to apply the current update to this record.

success.description: Confirm to finalize this workflow and keep the resulting state visible to the team.
success.title: Mark this workflow complete?

warning.description: Archiving removes this record from the active workflow while preserving its history.

items.actions: The footer always contains exactly two buttons: a neutral `secondary` cancel button and a confirm button in the semantic tone of the variant.
items.fixed: Alert dialog is a fixed confirmation surface. Do not use a free-form body, custom footer layouts, or arbitrary button combinations.
items.overhead: Variants control the overhead label: none for default, Positive Action for success, Cautionary Action for warning, and Destructive Action for danger.
items.severity: Use the variant to match the action tone: default, success, warning, or danger.

badge.description: Badges communicate compact status, category, or lifecycle metadata without taking over the layout. Use them when a short label should stay visually distinct inside cards, lists, and detail headers.

items.compact: Keep badge copy short. If the content needs a sentence, helper text, or multiple actions, it is no longer a badge.
items.status: Use badge for short status-like metadata such as active state, review stage, compact category, or risk level. It should stay compact and glanceable.
items.tone: Choose tone based on meaning, not decoration. The color should reinforce semantic state without carrying the entire message alone.
items.variant: Choose tone first and use variant only to tune emphasis inside the current surface. `primary` is solid in light mode and soft in dark mode. `secondary` is the outline treatment.

tones.description: Badges are most useful when they reinforce state or lifecycle meaning with compact, consistent visual treatment.
tones.title: Semantic tones

variants.description: Use `primary` for the default emphasized badge. It renders solid in light mode and soft in dark mode. Use `secondary` when the badge should stay outlined.
variants.title: Variant emphasis

badge.title: Badge

actionOrderCard.description: In multi-action rows, place the main action at the far right and keep supporting decisions to its left.
actionOrderCard.title: Right-most primary action

defaultCard.description: These examples show the stricter contract: default button, explicit primary, and the default grey secondary action.
defaultCard.title: Variant-driven defaults

button.description: Buttons trigger user actions. The contract is now stricter: `variant="primary"` is the main flat action, `variant="secondary"` is the lower-emphasis secondary action, and `usage` changes the color when the meaning changes.

items.actionOrder: In grouped actions, the primary action always belongs on the far right. Supporting and fallback actions stack to the left in decreasing emphasis.
items.default: The default button already defines the main contract: `variant="primary"`, `usage="primary"`, and `size="md"`. Use that baseline unless there is a clear reason to change emphasis.
items.icon: For icon-only buttons, always provide a readable `title`, `aria-label`, or `tooltipContent` so the component exposes an accessible name.
items.primaryVariant: Use `variant="primary"` for the main action in the set. Change `usage` only when the semantic color meaning changes, not to redefine the hierarchy of the button.
items.secondaryVariant: Use `variant="secondary"` for supporting or dismissive actions. Without an explicit `usage`, it should remain neutral and grey.

actionOrder.description: Primary actions stay on the far right. Secondary and fallback actions stack to the left in decreasing emphasis.
actionOrder.title: Action order

default.description: Start with the default primary button before reaching for overrides. Secondary intent should be expressed through the variant, not by ad hoc styling.
default.title: Default contract

primaryVariant.description: Primary variant is the filled action style. Keep it for the right-most main action and change `usage` only when the semantic intent changes.
primaryVariant.title: Primary variant colors

scale.description: Keep medium as the standard size. Move to small, large, or icon only when layout density truly requires it.
scale.title: Scale

secondaryVariant.description: Secondary variant is the secondary action style. It is the default fallback for lower-emphasis actions and supporting controls.
secondaryVariant.title: Secondary variant colors

states.description: Use the built-in states to keep feedback and interaction rules consistent.
states.title: States

actions.body: Keep footer actions tied to the content of that card. If actions belong to the entire page or section, place them outside the card instead.
actions.description: Some cards need a footer because they collect card-level decisions or compact metadata.
actions.title: Card with actions

summary.description: Cards can also hold compact dashboards and metadata without needing a footer.
summary.title: Summary card

card.description: Cards group related content into a bounded surface. Use them as layout containers for summaries, supporting actions, and content blocks that should read as a single unit.

compact.body: Use the footer for compact metadata such as badges or icons when the information still belongs to that single card.
compact.description: Cards do not need to be tall. Compact content works when the boundary still improves scanability.
compact.title: Compact marker

list.description: A card can contain multiple internal rows when they all belong to the same summary or task.

actions.description: If a row needs an action, it should still feel like part of the same card.
actions.title: Actions stay scoped

composition.description: Keep spacing and internal hierarchy consistent across all rows.
composition.title: Composition

overview.description: Use a shared surface when rows are part of the same conceptual block.
overview.title: Overview

list.title: Grouped list content

items.behavior: Cards are structural primitives. Behavior such as dialogs, tooltips, dropdowns, and navigation should come from the elements placed inside them, not from the card itself.
items.container: Use cards for one bounded unit such as a repeated item, compact summary, or contained tool. Do not use cards as page structure or arbitrary wrappers around everything.
items.footer: Card footer is for card-level actions or compact metadata only. If the card has neither, omit the footer entirely.
items.structure: Compose cards from header, description, content, and footer only when those slots are actually needed. Header descriptions should stay short, and the header icon belongs on the left through the `icon` prop.

header.body: If the body already carries enough context, do not force extra header copy just to fill space.
header.description: Use `CardHeader` when the content needs a visible title and short supporting description before the body. Pass the optional left icon through the header prop, not as manual child layout.

combobox.description: Combobox combines a trigger field with search and option picking. Use it when users must choose one value from a defined set but the list is long enough that search dramatically improves scanning.

items.metadata: Options may carry a short description or other supporting metadata, but the list should still remain fast to scan.
items.multiple: Multi-select mode works when users need several values from a large list and still benefit from search. Keep the trigger compact and rely on badges plus search rather than long inline text.
items.scope: Use it for people, places, categories, and other datasets where the user benefits from narrowing the list before choosing while still landing on one selected value.
items.search: Use combobox when search or filtering is part of the interaction. If the list is short and obvious, a normal select is usually enough.
items.single: Single-select remains the default mode. Use it when the workflow resolves to one chosen value.

items.constraints: Use `min` and `max` when one selected day must stay inside a known allowed window so the calendar communicates those bounds directly.
items.escalation: If the product later needs ranges, presets, recurring rules, or more complex timeline logic, that should become a higher-level composite component instead of stretching this baseline primitive.
items.native: Use this primitive when the product needs one calendar day and the field should still look and behave like the rest of the form system.
items.scope: The UI is date-only, but emitted values stay in backend-friendly datetime shape at midnight for the chosen day.

dialog.description: Dialogs interrupt the current flow to present focused content that needs attention without becoming a confirmation surface. Use them for notes, supporting details, and short focused reads tied to the current page.

basic.body: Use a dialog when the user needs to read or review focused supporting content without leaving the current page.
basic.title: Workspace notes

paragraphs.five: This keeps the layout stable while longer notes remain readable and bounded inside the same surface.
paragraphs.four: Longer reads are acceptable as long as the dialog still feels like supporting content instead of a full page squeezed into an overlay.
paragraphs.one: When content grows, only the body region should scroll. The header should remain fixed so the user keeps the title and close affordance in view.
paragraphs.six: If the content starts to demand editing flows, multi-step actions, or dense navigation, move it to another pattern instead of stretching the dialog.
paragraphs.three: The separator between header and body should stay visible so the content area reads as a distinct scrollable region.
paragraphs.two: Dialogs in this system do not use footer actions. They are for focused content, not confirmation hierarchies.

overhead.body: The optional overhead works well when the dialog belongs to a named pattern such as migration notes, reference context, or operational guidance.
overhead.title: Implementation guidance

overflowCard.description: Long content should stay bounded inside a vertically scrollable body while the header remains fixed.
overflowCard.title: Scrollable body

items.header: Do not use a subtitle below the title in this pattern. If extra context is needed, put it in the body or use the overhead as a small framing label above the title.
items.overflow: When content grows, only the body should scroll. The body uses the shared ScrollArea so the header remains visible.
items.structure: The structure is fixed: optional overhead, required title, top close button, separator, and required body. There is no footer.
items.useCase: Use dialog for focused supporting content, notes, and short reads. When the primary job is confirming an action, use `AlertDialog` instead of `Dialog`.

overflow.description: Long content should scroll inside the dialog body while the header remains fixed and the close affordance stays visible.
overflow.title: Overflow

structure.description: The dialog structure is fixed: optional overhead, required title, close button, separator, and required content body.
structure.title: $t(docs.card.sections.structure.title)

basic.description: A minimal dialog should provide a clear title and focused content without adding footer actions or descriptive subtitle copy.
basic.title: Default structure

overhead.description: Use overhead only when it adds a small framing label above the title, such as notes, reference, or guidance.
overhead.title: Optional overhead

dialog.title: Dialog

create.description: Use a right-side drawer for denser create flows that should stay tied to the current page context.
create.title: Create entity drawer

filters.description: Use the same right-side panel for richer filters when a small popover would become cramped or too fragmented.
filters.title: Heavy filters drawer

drawer.description: Drawers are the workflow overlay for heavier filters, creation flows, and denser subordinate work. In this system they are right-side panels with a structured header, scrollable body, and fixed task footer.

clear.description: Clearing removes all current values from the drawer body. This affects only the current draft.
clear.title: Clear this draft?

create.description: Set the initial record details before creating the new entity.

general.description: Name, classification, and the baseline values needed to open the record.
general.title: General data

ownership.description: Assign the responsible area and connect the entity to the correct campus context.

review.description: Keep a final compact review step when the creation flow contains more than one dense block.
review.title: Review before creation

create.title: $t(docs.drawer.examples.create.footer.action)

clear.description: Clearing removes the current filter values from this drawer and returns the panel to its initial state.
clear.title: Clear these filters?

filters.description: Adjust the active list view with broader filtering criteria without leaving the workspace.

advanced.description: Reserve the deeper accordion groups for less frequent criteria so the drawer stays scannable.
advanced.title: Filters

campus.description: Use secondary grouped controls for broader narrowing dimensions that do not need to stay visible all the time.
campus.title: Campus

status.description: Keep high-signal state filters near the top so the most common narrowing actions stay easy to reach.
status.title: Status

filters.title: Refine filters

items.body: The body is required and vertically scrollable through the shared ScrollArea. Use tabs for peer workflow sections and accordion for secondary disclosure inside the panel.
items.clear: The clear action should confirm through AlertDialog because it destroys local progress or filter setup.
items.context: Use drawer for heavy filtering, creation flows, and denser subordinate work that should remain attached to the current page.
items.escalation: If the surface becomes mostly informational, use Dialog instead. If it becomes simple anchored micro-interaction, reduce it to Popover.
items.footer: The footer is always present and task-oriented: clear on the left, primary action on the right. Do not place a close action in the footer.
items.header: The header supports overhead, required title, short description, and a top-right close button. Keep dismissal structural, not task-oriented.
items.primary: Use `info` for Apply filters and `success` for Create. The primary action should run directly; only destructive reset needs confirmation.
items.side: In this app, drawers are right-side panels. Keep the placement consistent instead of switching sides by feature.

<Drawer open={open} onOpenChange={setOpen}>

    <DrawerFooter
      actionVariant="filters"
      clearLabel="Clear filters"
      clearConfirmTitle="Clear these filters?"
      clearConfirmDescription="This removes the current filter values."
      actionLabel="Apply filters"

</Drawer>

dropdownMenu.description: Dropdown menus now follow one constrained pattern: compact row-action menus with icons on every item, a brand-accented first option, neutral supporting options, and an optional final semantic action group.

items.brandFirst: The first option is the primary row action and should use the brand accent. All regular supporting options stay neutral.
items.compact: Dropdown menu now follows one compact row-action pattern. Do not use this component as a generic navigation list or a menu for broad settings surfaces.
items.confirmation: Meaningful actions in the final group should open an `AlertDialog` before execution to confirm the user?s intent.
items.grouping: When a semantic final group exists, use only `DropdownMenuSuccessItem`, `DropdownMenuWarningItem`, and `DropdownMenuDangerItem`, in that order.
items.scope: Use dropdown menu for actions related to the current item, especially in lists, tables, and operational rows.

items.composition: Sections group content. Cards, tables, forms, and other dense surfaces belong inside `SectionContent` when they need more framing or behavior.
items.structure: Use sections to organize page-level content groups and introduce them with title and short description. Do not treat section itself as a decorative card.
items.surface: A section should usually remain visually quiet. If everything inside is one compact framed object, use a `Card` instead of forcing the section to become the surface.

items.clear: When a value is already selected, the trigger may expose direct clearing without reopening the panel, but the control still remains a single-value select.
items.forms: Select works well for compact forms and filters. If the content needs search, richer metadata, or multi-selection, move to another primitive.
items.grouping: Group options with labels and separators when the set mixes categories, areas, or lifecycle stages that benefit from lightweight hierarchy.
items.placeholder: Use a placeholder only when no initial selection should be implied. Once a value exists, the trigger should show it clearly.
items.scope: Use select when the user must choose one value from a defined set and the current value should stay visible in the field itself.

items.immediate: Switch should represent a live binary setting. If the user is choosing one option from a set, use radio group instead.
items.radio: Use radio group when exactly one option must be chosen from several mutually exclusive choices.
items.scope: Do not swap these controls casually. The chosen control should match the meaning of the decision, not just the amount of available space.
items.switch: Use switch for immediate on/off settings and binary preferences that read naturally as enabled or disabled.

items.manual: Use the low-level `Skeleton` primitive directly only when you need custom placeholder shapes outside the built-in card anatomy.
items.scope: Use skeletons in the same container that will eventually render the real content so spacing and hierarchy stay stable.
items.stability: Loading placeholders should preserve layout footprint. Avoid swapping between completely different structures while requests are in flight.

items.iconTabs: Icon-only tabs should use `TabsList variant="icon"` and expose `tooltipContent` on each trigger so the tab label remains discoverable.
items.label: Use label to associate form text with its field directly instead of relying on nearby typography alone.
items.labelSupport: Label belongs on inputs, text areas, comboboxes, and other fields that need a persistent visible name. Placeholder text does not replace that job.
items.scrollArea: Use `ScrollArea` in bounded regions such as sidebars, panels, dialogs, drawers, and dense lists. It needs a defined height or a real height cap for scrolling to happen.
items.scrollAreaBounded: Do not use `ScrollArea` as a wrapper for the whole page. It exists to contain overflow inside an already bounded surface.
items.scrollAreaViewport: Keep inner content padding and rhythm local to the content, but leave scrollbar and viewport behavior to the shared component.
items.separator: Use separators sparingly to reinforce grouping or rhythm. They should support hierarchy, not replace spacing and layout decisions.
items.separatorRhythm: Separator works best between related blocks, metadata rows, and section transitions. Do not use it as loose decoration.
items.tabs: Use tabs when several peer sections share the same space and the user needs to switch between them without leaving the route.

items.chrome: Use `colorVariant="chrome"` only on colored surfaces such as top bars and branded panels. Outside that context, keep the group on the default color variant.
items.defaultSurface: Use the single `Toggle` as the default pressed-action pattern on neutral surfaces. It should not turn into a substitute for checkbox, radio group, or switch.
items.group: Use `ToggleGroup` when several related states need to behave as one coherent set, whether exclusive or multi-pressed.
items.pill: Use the `pill` variant when the items should read as one connected segmented control with no gaps between parts.
items.press: These controls communicate pressed state, not form semantics. Prefer checkbox, radio group, or switch when the meaning is selection or settings.
items.scope: Toggle controls are strongest in toolbars, view switches, formatting actions, and compact filter chips.
items.spaced: Use the `spaced` variant for toolbars, view switches, and formatting groups where each item should remain clearly separate.
items.toggle: Use toggle for one pressed/unpressed control that behaves like a stateful action button.

chrome.description: Use the chrome color variant when the group sits on a branded or colored background.
chrome.title: Chrome on colored surfaces

default.description: This is the baseline pressed-action control for neutral surfaces.
default.title: Default single toggle

pill.description: The pill variant turns the group into one connected control for compact mutually exclusive choices.
pill.title: Pill group variant

spaced.description: The default toggle group layout keeps items separated. Use it for toolbars, formatting sets, and view switches.
spaced.title: Spaced group variant

tooltip.description: Tooltips provide short contextual hints for controls and inline UI elements. Use the wrapper directly around custom triggers or rely on automatic tooltips for icon-only buttons.

items.clarify: Use tooltips to clarify compact controls, especially icon-only actions that need visible context on hover or focus.
items.compact: Keep tooltip copy short and neutral. If the content needs instructions or multiple actions, use another pattern such as helper text, a popover, or a dialog.
items.iconButtons: For icon buttons, the button primitive already resolves tooltip content from `tooltipContent`, `title`, or the accessible label.
items.reinforce: Tooltips should reinforce an existing control, not carry essential information by themselves.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Tooltip content="Open details" side="top" align="center">
  <button type="button">Trigger</button>
</Tooltip>

<Button
  size="icon"
  variant="secondary"
  title="Add item"
  leadingIcon={<Plus />}
/>
